summary: Gruber dismantles two common defenses of Apple's rejection of the Podcaster app from the App Store. He argues that the SDK Agreement section cited by Daniel Dilger clearly refers to application distribution, not content distribution, and that numerous already-approved apps contradict that interpretation. He also rejects the bandwidth argument, noting Apple's own YouTube app and other approved apps stream freely over cellular. Gruber concludes that Podcaster complies with every written guideline and that Apple's rejection is arbitrary and unjustified. He draws a sharp distinction between what Apple can do and what it should do.
key_points: Section 3.3.3 of the SDK Agreement prohibits distributing app features outside iTunes, not content — podcasts are neither 'additional features' nor 'functionality'
Multiple approved apps (AOL Radio, Pandora, FileMagnet, Air Sharing) already distribute audio content outside iTunes, contradicting the cited justification
The bandwidth argument fails because Apple's own YouTube app and MLB At Bat stream video over cellular, and Apple could have simply required Wi-Fi-only mode
Podcaster downloads free content, so even a revenue-protection rationale doesn't apply
The core issue is not Apple's legal right to reject apps but whether they should exercise that right arbitrarily
money_quotes: This is such bullshit it hurts my head.
Podcast downloads are neither 'additional features' nor 'functionality'.
Podcaster, so far as I can tell, complies with every written guideline in the SDK Agreement.
If other guidelines exist, Apple should add them to the agreement.
The point isn't about what Apple can do but what they should do.
And they shouldn't be doing this.
key_insight: Apple's rejection of Podcaster cannot be justified by any written SDK guideline or practical concern, exposing the danger of arbitrary App Store gatekeeping even when developers play by the rules.
